learn.title: Factorials
learn.intro: Factorials show up everywhere from counting problems to trailing-zero puzzles. The one formula worth memorizing cold — Legendre's trailing-zero count — turns intimidating-looking questions into simple arithmetic.

sections.explanation: The factorial of a number n (written n!) is the product of all positive integers from 1 to n. Factorials grow extremely fast and are used in counting/permutation problems as well as trailing-zero and divisibility puzzles.
sections.realLifeExample: The number of ways to arrange 5 different books on a shelf is 5!=120 — factorials directly count arrangements like this.
sections.memoryTrick: Factorial explodes fast: 5!=120, but 10! is already over 3.6 million.
sections.shortcutTrick: To find trailing zeros in n!, count how many times 5 divides into the numbers from 1 to n (there are always more factors of 2 than 5): trailing zeros = floor(n/5) + floor(n/25) + floor(n/125) + ...
formulaCard: Trailing zeros in n! = floor(n/5) + floor(n/25) + floor(n/125) + ...
commonMistakes: Counting only floor(n/5) for trailing zeros and forgetting the higher powers of 5 (25, 125...) once n is large enough.
sections.talentSprintTip: TalentSprint often asks 'how many trailing zeros in 100!' — always check whether n is large enough to need the floor(n/25) and floor(n/125) terms too.
sections.tcsNqtTip: TCS NQT sometimes asks for the highest power of a prime p (like 2 or 3) dividing n! — use the same formula: floor(n/p) + floor(n/p²) + ...

solvedExamples.question: Find the number of trailing zeros in 25!.
Find the number of trailing zeros in 100!.
Find the highest power of 2 that divides 10!.

practiceExamples.question: Find the number of trailing zeros in 50!.
Find the number of trailing zeros in 30!.
Find the highest power of 3 dividing 15!.

easy.question: What is 5!?
easy.shortcut: Build up from 4!=24 by multiplying by 5.

easy.question: What is 0!?
easy.explanation: By definition, 0! = 1 — a fixed convention in mathematics, not something computed from a product.
whyWrong: 0 is a common but incorrect guess — 0! is defined as 1.
10 doesn't relate to the definition at all.

easy.question: Simplify 6!/4!.
whyWrong: 24 is 4! itself, not the simplified ratio.
easy.shortcut: n!/(n−2)! = n×(n−1) — cancel the common factorial tail directly.

easy.question: How many trailing zeros does 10! have?
easy.explanation: 10! = 3,628,800, and floor(10/5)=2 confirms exactly 2 trailing zeros.
whyWrong: 0 is incorrect since 10! clearly ends in 00.
easy.shortcut: Trailing zeros = floor(n/5) for n below 25, since floor(n/25)=0 in that range.

easy.question: Which of these is the correct value of 4!?
easy.shortcut: Memorize factorials up to 7! for instant recall.

medium.question: Find the number of trailing zeros in 20!.
medium.explanation: floor(20/5)=4, and floor(20/25)=0, so 20! has exactly 4 trailing zeros.
medium.shortcut: For n<25, trailing zeros = floor(n/5) alone.

medium.question: Simplify 9!/7!.
medium.shortcut: n!/(n−2)! = n×(n−1) — no need to compute both full factorials.

medium.question: Find the highest power of 2 dividing 12!.
whyWrong: 8 undercounts — it misses contributions from higher powers of 2.
medium.shortcut: Sum floor(n/2)+floor(n/4)+floor(n/8)+... until the divisor exceeds n.

medium.question: How many zeros are at the end of 15!?
medium.explanation: floor(15/5)=3, and floor(15/25)=0, giving exactly 3 trailing zeros.

medium.question: Simplify (n+2)!/n! in terms of n.
medium.explanation: (n+2)!/n! cancels the common n! tail, leaving (n+2)(n+1).
whyWrong: n(n+1) doesn't correctly represent the two terms above n! in the numerator.
medium.shortcut: (n+k)!/n! always equals the product of the k terms counting down from (n+k): (n+1)(n+2)...(n+k).

medium.question: If n! = 5040, find n.
medium.shortcut: Memorize factorials up to 8! to answer these instantly.

medium.question: Find the number of trailing zeros in 40!.
whyWrong: 8 misses the extra contribution from floor(40/25)=1.
medium.shortcut: Always check floor(n/25) once n reaches or exceeds 25, not just floor(n/5).

medium.question: Simplify 100!/98!.
medium.shortcut: n!/(n−2)! = n×(n−1) works even for large n.

medium.question: Find the units digit of 15!.
medium.explanation: Any factorial n! for n≥5 contains both 2 and 5 as factors, making it divisible by 10 — so its units digit is always 0.
whyWrong: 5 is incorrect — the presence of a factor of 2 alongside 5 always produces a trailing 0, not a bare 5.
2 doesn't match the units digit of any factorial ≥5!.
medium.shortcut: Any factorial from 5! onward always ends in 0 — no calculation needed for the units digit.

medium.question: Find the number of trailing zeros in 5! + 10!.
medium.explanation: 5!=120 and 10!=3,628,800. Their sum is 3,628,920, which ends in only one zero (the digit before it is 2) — the total has exactly 1 trailing zero, not the smaller individual count.
whyWrong: 2 would be true if the sum's trailing zeros equalled the minimum of the two addends' zero counts, but addition doesn't work that way.
0 undercounts — the sum does end in at least one zero.
medium.shortcut: When adding numbers with trailing zeros, compute the actual sum's last few digits directly — trailing zero counts don't simply combine by taking the minimum.

hard.question: Find the number of trailing zeros in 125!.
whyWrong: 30 undercounts by missing the floor(125/125)=1 contribution.
hard.shortcut: Keep dividing n by increasing powers of 5 (5, 25, 125, 625...) until the quotient is 0, then sum all the quotients.

hard.question: Find the highest power of 5 dividing 50!.
hard.shortcut: Legendre's formula works for any prime — sum floor(n/pᵏ) for k=1,2,3...

hard.question: If n! has exactly 2 trailing zeros, what is the largest possible value of n?
hard.explanation: floor(n/5)=2 for n=10 through 14. At n=15, floor(15/5)=3, so the count jumps. The largest n still giving exactly 2 is 14.
whyWrong: 10 is the smallest n giving 2 trailing zeros, not the largest.
15 already gives 3 trailing zeros, one too many.
12 does give 2 trailing zeros but isn't the largest such n — 13 and 14 also work.
hard.shortcut: Trailing zero count only increases at multiples of 5 — the count stays constant for a run of consecutive n right before the next multiple of 5.

hard.question: Find the number of trailing zeros in 23!.
hard.explanation: floor(23/5)=4, and floor(23/25)=0, giving exactly 4 trailing zeros.
hard.shortcut: For n<25, trailing zeros = floor(n/5) alone.

hard.question: Simplify n!/(n−3)! in terms of n.
hard.explanation: n!/(n−3)! cancels the common (n−3)! tail, leaving the product of the 3 terms above it: n(n−1)(n−2).
whyWrong: (n−1)(n−2)(n−3) shifts the terms down by one position incorrectly.
n(n−1) only accounts for 2 terms, not 3.
n(n−1)(n−2)(n−3) includes one extra term beyond what cancels.
hard.shortcut: n!/(n−k)! always equals the product of the k terms counting down from n.

hard.question: Find the number of trailing zeros in the product 15! × 20!.
hard.explanation: Unlike addition, trailing zeros DO add when multiplying: 15! contributes 3 (floor(15/5)=3) and 20! contributes 4 (floor(20/5)=4), so their product has 3+4=7 trailing zeros.
whyWrong: 3 only accounts for 15!'s zeros, ignoring 20!'s contribution.
hard.shortcut: When multiplying numbers, their trailing zero counts always add directly — unlike addition, where this shortcut doesn't apply.

hard.question: What is the smallest n such that n! is divisible by 1000?
hard.explanation: 1000=2³×5³. Since factors of 5 are always the limiting factor in a factorial, we need floor(n/5)≥3, which first happens at n=15 (floor(14/5)=2 is insufficient).
whyWrong: 10 gives floor(10/5)=2 factors of 5, one short of the required 3.
20 works too, but isn't the smallest such n.
hard.shortcut: For 'n! divisible by 10^k' questions, find the smallest n where floor(n/5)≥k — factors of 2 are never the bottleneck.

hard.question: Find the unit digit of 1! + 2! + 3! + 4! + 5!.
whyWrong: 0 doesn't match — this short sum genuinely ends in 3, not 0.
5 doesn't match the actual sum's last digit.
hard.shortcut: For a short sum like this (only up to 5!), just add the factorials directly — the 'ignore terms from 5! onward' shortcut applies only to much longer sums going well past 5!.

hard.question: Find the number of trailing zeros in 10! × 15!.
hard.explanation: Trailing zeros of a product equal the sum of each factor's trailing zeros: 10! contributes 2, 15! contributes 3, totaling 5.
whyWrong: 2 only accounts for 10!'s zeros, ignoring 15!'s contribution.
hard.shortcut: Trailing zeros add across a product — compute each factor's zero count separately, then sum them.

hard.question: If n! ends in exactly 6 zeros, find the range of possible values of n.
hard.explanation: floor(n/5)+floor(n/25)=6 holds for n=25 through 29 (at n=30, the count jumps to 7 since floor(30/5)=6 combined with floor(30/25)=1 gives 7).
whyWrong: 20 to 24 gives only 4 trailing zeros, two short.
30 to 34 already gives 7 trailing zeros, one too many.
24 to 28 straddles the boundary incorrectly — 24 gives only 4 zeros while 25-28 give 6, so the range isn't uniformly 6.
hard.shortcut: Trailing zero counts stay constant across a run of consecutive n values between multiples of 5 (and jump further at multiples of 25) — find the exact boundary values first.

quickSheet: Trailing zeros in n! = floor(n/5)+floor(n/25)+floor(n/125)+... (keep going until the divisor exceeds n).
n!/(n−k)! = the product of the k terms counting down from n — never compute both full factorials separately.
Trailing zeros ADD when multiplying factorials, but do NOT simply combine when adding them — always check the actual sum for addition questions.
Any factorial from 5! onward always ends in 0.
oneMinute: Memorize factorials up to 8! (40320) for instant recall.
Trailing zero count = count the factors of 5, not 2 (5 is always the bottleneck).
The count stays constant for a run of n values, jumping only at multiples of 5, 25, 125...
revision.summary: Factorial questions in placement exams almost always reduce to one core skill: counting how many times 5 divides into a range of numbers (Legendre's formula), since 5 is always the bottleneck for trailing zeros. Combine that with knowing n!/(n−k)! cancels down to a short product, and you can handle nearly every factorial question without ever computing a full factorial.
